issue_title: Remove scheduler timeouts / Taking too long to pull a run container fails with 'failed to create container'
issue_body:
Debugging Deis on Azure led us to initially suspect an Azure-specific issue, but we have seen this on other platforms. Any time a `deis run` takes longer than 60 seconds to complete the `docker pull`, the client errs with `failed to create container`.

This leads to a deeper issue with our timeouts -- the router uses a 20 minute timeout (https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/router/image/templates/nginx.conf#L152-L153). In the fleet scheduling logic, we have various loops which perform actions:

* [_wait_for_container](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L163)
* [_wait_for_destroy](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L180)
* [run](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L216)
* [wait for launch](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L260)
* [wait for start](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L269)
* [wait for completion](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L282)

We really shouldn't be "budgeting" the 20 minutes across these timeouts, as we have no idea how long any one will take at any one time. For example, on Azure, a `docker pull` for my run container took 5 minutes, but our pollwait loop only waits for 60s. Letting each of these loops run indefinitely and instead letting the worker/router handle the total request timeout is probably preferred here.

This may be related to #3819, but am thinking not because I can deploy without a problem.

Recently, I upgraded from 1.2.x to 1.6.1 and then today from 1.6.1 to 1.7.0 (using the "in-place upgrade" instructions for both). Alas, despite being able to deploy without problem, all of my apps now 404 (Including Deis' own example-nodejs-express, which I just deployed).

`fleetctl list-units` shows everything up. Looking through the logs for the router doesn't reveal anything obvious, except the following, which might have been while I was upgrading:

```
Jun 09 12:18:19 ip-10-21-2-14.eu-west-1.compute.internal sh[2486]: [INFO] - 2015/06/09 12:18:19 [emerg] 742#0: invalid port in upstream "10.21.1.64:" in /opt/nginx/conf/.nginx.conf159700271:164
Jun 09 12:18:19 ip-10-21-2-14.eu-west-1.compute.internal sh[2486]: 2015-06-09T12:18:19Z 9cffb69233b8 confd[47]: ERROR Config check failed: exit status 1
```

Yet if I do `deis logs`, it seems the application is up and running and everything's fine.

Any suggestions?

This has been attempted as two different approaches (in #2774 by @aledbf and in #2772 by @PierreKircher). We need to agree on an implementation so the folks interested in helping can coordinate effectively. Specifically:
* What are we using for service discovery? SkyDns?
* How is the service discovery component shipped?

We'll need to come up with an approach here that everyone agrees with.

issue_title: Proposal: Enable opt-in HTTPS between router and app containers
issue_body:
See issue #4611 

Deis currently uses "mullet TLS--" security in the front, party in the back.  Having come from healthcare, I understand all too well how this rules out the possibility of using Deis to host any workloads that deal with PHI.  This is a gap that needs to be closed.

This issue isn't meant to duplicate #4611, but rather to serve as a forum for discussing proposed designs that address this.  I'll start by throwing my proposal into the ring.  Feedback is welcome, as are alternate proposals.

Approach
-------------

I think we can pretty easily go 80/20 on this and get ourselves most of the way to where we need to be pretty quickly and then improve incrementally from there going forward.  Initially, I'd propose that we support the the following, at minimum:

* 100% opt-in on an application basis so as not to impact existing, deployed applications upon platform upgrade.
* _Option_ for end-to-end HTTPS needs to be there.  If when selecting this option, it proves infeasible to _close_ the HTTP route into the same application in the first iteration, so be it.
* Self-signed certificates are good enough, initially.  It's only the router that needs to trust the certs-- not the end user's agent.

Possible implementation
---------------------------------

I believe operators should opt-in to this on an application basis by setting an environment variable via `deis config:set`.

The following is all WRT to buildpack-based applications:

One possible implementation is to generate a self-signed certificate at build-time.  I imagine the easiest way of doing this being modifications to the slugbuilder such that after the buildpack(s) have generated the application's deployable artifacts, the generated cert and key are added to those right before compressing those artifacts into a slug (tarball).  A corresponding change to the slugrunner can start Nginx as a proxy in front of the application to terminate TLS.

Open questions and concerns
----------------------------------------

I think experimentation is the best way to gain insight into the following:

* Will application containers _all_ include a self-signed cert and Nginx?  Or just those that have opted into it?  This depends on the availability of relevant information (environment variable) at build time.  Fortunately, the environment variables for each application are _also_ inserted into etcd, so we probably do have access to this information at build-time.

* If one opts into end-to-end HTTPS, can we expose the secure port in the application containers _instead of_ the insecure port?  Or, initially, do we expose it _in addition to_ the insecure port?  Proposing we shoot for the former; settle for the latter if necessary (for the first iteration).

* How are routing and publishing affected?  Publishing may or may not be affected depending on the previous bullet.  Routing rules will be slightly different for anyone any app that opts into this.

* How does this affect health checks and liveliness checks?

* How does it work with k8s as the scheduler?  With k8s as the scheduler, publisher isn't really relevant.  Router proxies service URLs.  Those service URLs likely need to change for any app that has opted into this.

* How does this work for developers pushing their own Dockerfile or for those using `deis pull`?  My personal take is that anyone who's already exercising that level of control over their images is responsible for exposing the secure endpoint themselves.  We would just need to clearly document how the router plugs into that in such scenarios.
deisctl --version should return an exit code of 0

I saw #3884 but it looks like it was closed. I'm running 1.7.3 in AWS.

We enabled healthchecks for a staging instance of our bedrock application, and are still seeing downtime during deploys.

I wrote a [script](https://gist.github.com/jgmize/6175f697259a9df5c6f2) to observe the cluster while making a configuration change to trigger a deploy, and captured the [output](https://gist.github.com/jgmize/674d0c2ea03cbb96971f). What I observed was the following:

1. The previous version (v128) of the app (bedrock-stage) stops being published to the /deis/services/bedrock-stage before the new version (v129) has entered the running state. https://gist.github.com/jgmize/674d0c2ea03cbb96971f#file-watch-bedrock-stage-L411-L452
2. When the routers refresh their config, the app stops being published and all requests begin to return a 404 https://gist.github.com/jgmize/674d0c2ea03cbb96971f#file-watch-bedrock-stage-L473-L637
3. The publisher begins publishing v128 to etcd under /deis/services/bedrock-stage/ again (https://gist.github.com/jgmize/674d0c2ea03cbb96971f#file-watch-bedrock-stage-L638-L1009), and then shortly begins publishing v129 as well (https://gist.github.com/jgmize/674d0c2ea03cbb96971f#file-watch-bedrock-stage-L1010-L2137)
4. v128 continues to be published even after the units have stopped running according to fleet (https://gist.github.com/jgmize/674d0c2ea03cbb96971f#file-watch-bedrock-stage-L2138-L2196) (this should be less of an issue than the original problem due to the proxy_next_upstream in the nginx.conf)
5. After a few more seconds, the nginx.conf is updated to reflect the values in etcd and begins routing traffic to the new instances exclusively (https://gist.github.com/jgmize/674d0c2ea03cbb96971f#file-watch-bedrock-stage-L2247-L2266)

Note that this does not happen every time, making tracking the race condition down difficult, but we are seeing intermittent downtime during deploys in both of our clusters.

issue_title: Missing Quotes around Dickey Fuller attr parameter
issue_body:
Oh no, you encountered a problem while using *tsfesh*.

We, the maintainers, are happy to help you. When opening an issue, please provide the following information to us:

1. Your operating system
macOS 10.12.6
2. The version of *tsfresh* that you are using
0.10.0
3. The data on which the problem occurred (please do not upload 1000s of time series but try to boil the problem down to a small group or even a singular one)
UCI har data 
4. A minimal code snippet which reproduces the problem/bug
On _calculate_relevance_table_, it returns a dataframe containing a column of the feature calculators with parameters. Some features accept string parameters and _calculate_relevance_table_ returns them surrounded in double quotes: 
> z_earth_user__agg_linear_trend__f_agg_"var"__chunk_len_50__attr_"slope"

however for the _augmented_dickey_fuller_ calcuator, the string param is not surrounded by quotes

> z_earth_user__augmented_dickey_fuller__attr_pvalue

the quotes are necessary for get_config_from_string. If the string param doesn't have the double quotes it will raise a _ValueError: malformed string_ on _ast.iteral_eval(value)_

> File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/tsfresh/feature_extraction/settings.py", line 65, in from_columns 
>    config = get_config_from_string(parts)

>    return _convert(node_or_string) 
>  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/ast.py", line 79, in _convert 
>    raise ValueError('malformed string')

>

issue_title: Feature extraction support for time series with different sampling rates
issue_body:
In a scenario with time series with different sampling rates, a dataset could look like:
```
	id	time	a	b
0	1	0	4	9.0
1	1	1	3	NaN
2	1	2	5	7.0
3	1	3	7	NaN
4	2	0	8	13.0
5	2	1	11	NaN
6	2	2	4	11.0
7	2	3	2	NaN
```
The sampling frequency of `a` is 1 and of `b` is 0.5.

tsfresh does not allow this timeseries, because the column `b` contains `NaN` values.
```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from tsfresh import extract_features

data = {
    "id": [1,1,1,1,2,2,2,2],
    "time": [0,1,2,3,0,1,2,3],
    "a": [4,3,5,7,8,11,4,2],
    "b": [9,np.nan,7,np.nan,13,np.nan,11,np.nan],
}
columns = ["id", "time", "a", "b"]
timeseries = pd.DataFrame(data=data, columns=columns)

extract_features(timeseries, column_id='id', column_sort='time')
```
Output:
```
/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tsfresh/utilities/dataframe_functions.py in normalize_input_to_internal_representation(df_or_dict, column_id, column_sort, column_kind, column_value)
    324     for kind in kind_to_df_map:
    325         if kind_to_df_map[kind][column_value].isnull().any():
--> 326             raise ValueError("You have NaN values in your value column.")
    327 
    328     return kind_to_df_map, column_id, column_value

ValueError: You have NaN values in your value column.
```
At the moment, in that case, the dataset must be split into two seperate sets:
```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from tsfresh import extract_features
from tsfresh.feature_extraction import MinimalFeatureExtractionSettings

settings = MinimalFeatureExtractionSettings()

data_a = {
    "id": [1,1,1,1,2,2,2,2],
    "time": [0,1,2,3,0,1,2,3],
    "a": [4,3,5,7,8,11,4,2],
}
data_b = {
    "id": [1,1,2,2],
    "time": [0,2,0,2],
    "b": [9,7,13,11],
}
columns_a = ["id", "time", "a"]
columns_b = ["id", "time", "b"]

timeseries_a = pd.DataFrame(data=data_a, columns=columns_a)
timeseries_b = pd.DataFrame(data=data_b, columns=columns_b)

features_a = extract_features(timeseries_a, column_id='id', column_sort='time', feature_extraction_settings=settings)
features_b = extract_features(timeseries_b, column_id='id', column_sort='time', feature_extraction_settings=settings)

pd.concat([features_a, features_b], axis=1)
```

It would be nice to implement support for timeseries with different sampling rates, which in my case are generated by SQL queries including multiple tables.
What I can imagine is the introduction of a new parameter (`has_different_sampling_rates`) to the function `extract_features()` and if it is set to `True` then allow `NaN` values. To extract features with only valid values, each column could be filtered with `column.loc[~column.isnull()]` before feeding them to the calculators.

This modification would also allow different timeseries with equal sampling rates but shifted timestamps:
```
	id	time	a	b
0	1	0	NaN	9.0
1	1	0.1	3	NaN
2	1	1	NaN	7.0
3	1	1.1	7	NaN
```

issue_title: Switch to `BinaryTrie` for transactions and receipts
issue_body:
### What is wrong?
For sharding, we switch all `HexaryTrie` to `BinaryTrie`.

### How can it be fixed
Most changes are in `evm.db.chain.BaseChainDB`. I suggest we divide it into two stages:
1. `master` branch: Make `trie_class` to be configurable in `BaseChainDB.__init__`.
2. Rebase `sharding` branch and switch sharding-related tries to `BinaryTrie`.

issue_title: Calling state-chaning function of Smart Contract and wait till transaction is mined
issue_body:
* Version: 4.0.0
* Python: 3.5
* OS: Ubuntu 16


### What was wrong?
I am currently using the Quickstart code and at the end we are calling the setGreeting function of the contract. I would like to know how it is possible to get the tx hash (as with contract.deploy) so that I can wait until the transaction is mined.

```
import json
import web3

from web3 import Web3, HTTPProvider, TestRPCProvider
from solc import compile_source
from web3.contract import ConciseContract

# Solidity source code
contract_source_code = '''
pragma solidity ^0.4.0;

contract Greeter {
    string public greeting;

    function Greeter() {
        greeting = 'Hello';
    }

    function setGreeting(string _greeting) public {
        greeting = _greeting;
    }

    function greet() constant returns (string) {
        return greeting;
    }
}
'''

compiled_sol = compile_source(contract_source_code) # Compiled source code
contract_interface = compiled_sol['<stdin>:Greeter']

# web3.py instance
w3 = Web3(TestRPCProvider())

# Instantiate and deploy contract
contract = w3.eth.contract(abi=contract_interface['abi'], bytecode=contract_interface['bin'])

# Get transaction hash from deployed contract
tx_hash = contract.deploy(transaction={'from': w3.eth.accounts[0], 'gas': 410000})

# Get tx receipt to get contract address
tx_receipt = w3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
contract_address = tx_receipt['contractAddress']

# Contract instance in concise mode
abi = contract_interface['abi']
contract_instance = w3.eth.contract(address=contract_address, abi=abi,ContractFactoryClass=ConciseContract)

# Getters + Setters for web3.eth.contract object
print('Contract value: {}'.format(contract_instance.greet()))
contract_instance.setGreeting('Nihao', transact={'from': w3.eth.accounts[0]})
print('Setting value to: Nihao')
print('Contract value: {}'.format(contract_instance.greet()))
```

@daler commented on [Wed Oct 12 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1454)

I think `conda-build` is reporting the wrong thing when it hits a certain kind of dependency conflict. This gets kind of complicated so I'll first describe it in general terms and then show a reproducible example.
## The general problem
- A depends on B and C
- B depends on C
- A is to be built for `CONDA_R=3.3.1`
- B only exists in channels for `CONDA_R=3.2`. So `conda-build` should report a dependency conflict when building A
- C exists in channels for `CONDA_R=3.3.1` and `CONDA_R=3.2`

When I try building A, I expect to get a dependency conflict error saying that B doesn't exist for `R=3.3.1`. But instead I get a dependency conflict error saying that C doesn't exist for `R=3.3.1` (when in fact it does).
## Working example
### `conda info` on relevant packages

This package `bioconductor-annotationdbi` (package "C" in the example above), is built for both R3.2 and R3.3:

```
bioconductor-annotationdbi 1.32.3 0
-----------------------------------
file name   : bioconda::bioconductor-annotationdbi-1.32.3-0.tar.bz2
dependencies:
    bioconductor-biobase >=1.17.0
    bioconductor-biocgenerics >=0.15.10
    bioconductor-iranges
    bioconductor-s4vectors
    r >=2.7.0
    r-dbi
    r-rsqlite


bioconductor-annotationdbi 1.34.4 r3.3.1_1
------------------------------------------
file name   : bioconda::bioconductor-annotationdbi-1.34.4-r3.3.1_1.tar.bz2
dependencies:
    bioconductor-biobase >=1.17.0
    bioconductor-biocgenerics >=0.15.10
    bioconductor-iranges
    bioconductor-s4vectors >=0.9.25
    r 3.3.1*
    r-dbi
    r-rsqlite
```

This package `bioconductor-genomicfeatures` ("B" in the example above) is not yet built for R3.3.1. Note that it has a dependency for `bioconductor-annotationdbi`:

```
bioconductor-genomicfeatures 1.22.13 r3.2.2_0
---------------------------------------------
file name   : bioconda::bioconductor-genomicfeatures-1.22.13-r3.2.2_0.tar.bz2
dependencies:
    bioconductor-annotationdbi >=1.27.9
    bioconductor-biobase >=2.15.1
    bioconductor-biocgenerics >=0.1.0
    bioconductor-biomart >=2.17.1
    bioconductor-biostrings >=2.23.3
    bioconductor-genomeinfodb >=1.5.16
    bioconductor-genomicranges >=1.21.32
    bioconductor-iranges >=2.3.21
    bioconductor-rtracklayer >=1.29.24
    bioconductor-s4vectors >=0.7.17
    bioconductor-xvector
    r 3.2.2*
    r-dbi
    r-rcurl
    r-rsqlite
```
### the problematic `meta.yaml`

This `meta.yaml` (package "A" in the example) illustrates the problem.

``` yaml
package:
  name: demo
  version: 0.1
requirements:
  build:
    - bioconductor-annotationdbi
    - bioconductor-genomicfeatures
    - r
```

When I try building it, it says that `bioconductor-annotationdbi` conflicts:

```
$ conda build . --channel r --channel bioconda
BUILD START: demo-0.1-0
updating index in: /miniconda3/conda-bld/linux-64
updating index in: /miniconda3/conda-bld/noarch
Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org
...INFO:stderrlog:...
Can't build /bioconda-recipes/tmp due to unsatisfiable dependencies:
The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - bioconductor-annotationdbi
  - r 3.3.1*
Use "conda info <package>" to see the dependencies for each package.
```

I interpreted this to mean that `bioconductor-annotationdbi` does not exist for R3.3.1. But in fact it does -- and it's even found by `conda info`.

Curiously, if I comment out the line for `bioconductor-genomicfeatures`, it works. Note that I'm commenting out the package _not_ listed as a dependency conflict:

``` yaml
package:
  name: demo
  version: 0.1
requirements:
  build:
    - bioconductor-annotationdbi
    # - bioconductor-genomicfeatures
    - r
```

```
$ conda build . --channel r --channel bioconda
...
# Automatic uploading is disabled
# If you want to upload this package to anaconda.org later, type:
#
# $ anaconda upload /miniconda3/conda-bld/linux-64/demo-0.1-0.tar.bz2
#
# To have conda build upload to anaconda.org automatically, use
# $ conda config --set anaconda_upload yes

```
### Finally:

So when `conda-build` reported the conflict:

```
The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - bioconductor-annotationdbi
  - r 3.3.1*
```

shouldn't it instead be reporting that `bioconductor-genomicfeatures` is the conflicting package?

---

issue_title: Fallback procedure to add to conda's repertoire
issue_body:
If a package is pip-installable, why would it be unavailable on conda? For example: **boto3** which is slowly replacing *boto* heavily used for Amazon AWS. 

Currently conda suggests a search on anaconda.org -- however, to use an offered package from a random user poses security risk for code such as boto3 which transmits credentials. 

What would the difficulty be in having conda call pip internally for package installation, so that the client does not have to set up a virtualenv environment. Also it is not clear whether one should use pip within a conda environment without breaking its infrastructure. 

Once explained, I would be most happy to update the documentation, or modify the exception case when a package is not within conda's reach. This must be a frustration or FAQ for many.

issue_title: _'s in versions are not accepted by conda 4.3
issue_body:
I think this needs to be considered a blocker for 4.3 as it means that environments which were previously considered OK are now not OK.

When `conda skeleton cran` fetches r-packages from CRAN it replaces any `-` in the version with `_` (and helpfully puts a comment to that effect in the recipe), e.g.:

```
package:
  name: r-bit
  # Note that conda versions cannot contain -, so any -'s in the version have
  # been replaced with _'s.
  version: "1.1_12"
```

and also in the dependencies we also see such versions:

```
requirements:
  build:
    - r-base
    - r-bit >=1.1_12
```

Now, when conda is asked to install such dependencies I am getting:
```
failed to parse packages from exception: Value error: invalid package specification: r-bit=>=1.1_12
```

`conda install -c r r-irkernel`

```
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    r-irkernel: 0.3-r3.1.3_0

    libpng:     1.5.13-1 --> 1.6.17-0

Updating `libpng` is problematic for me, since it would break all packages that are still linked to the old `libpng` library.

Is there any workaround for that? Can I somehow keep multiple `libpng` versions around?

issue_title: Problem: conda-build doesn't clean up temporary files
issue_body:
@gabm commented on [Thu Aug 17 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/2289)

It seems conda-build doesn't clean up temporary files located under `/tmp/tmpxxxxxx` or `User/../AppData/Local/tmpxxxxxx` on windows. After the build these folders contain the decompressed packages that I suppose have been installed as dependencies during the build. Maybe its also a conda problem?!

Anyway, on our Windows build machine we accumulated 25Gb within some weeks .... 

---

@gabm commented on [Thu Aug 17 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/2289#issuecomment-323134590)

.. the same files are located again in `miniconda3/pkgs`... 

---

@msarahan commented on [Thu Aug 17 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/2289#issuecomment-323158750)

Conda-build uses this functionality in conda for temporary folders: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/dbe99de1d45206c5d6f2e19017a9e2466f81dc6d/conda/compat.py#L23-L67

When we get files from packages, we do so with the package_has_file function: https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/master/conda_build/utils.py#L714-L730

package_has_file does not ever extract the whole package.  This seems like a conda issue.  Sorry, I don't currently have time to investigate this more.  I can move this issue to the conda repo if you'd like.



---

@gabm commented on [Thu Aug 17 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/2289#issuecomment-323170508)

ok, then please move it there... If I find time, I'll have a further look. I realised some strange folders `/tmp/tmpxxxxxxx` some weeks ago, so could be easy to reproduce...

issue_title: conda.api.get_index not using the platform kwarg
issue_body: In tracking down a particularly fiddly bug with conda-smithy (https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-smithy/pull/274) I noticed that `conda.api.get_index` is no longer using the platform kwarg - it is now exclusively coming from `conda.config.subdir`. That is fine, but I just wanted to highlight the issue, and raise awareness of the value of being able to get an index for any platform (in testing, and in resolving how an environment should look).

issue_title: conda install python=3.5 leaves environment broken
issue_body:
I wanted to upgrade my conda installation from Python 3.4 to 3.5. I used `conda install python=3.5` as suggested [in the docs](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/py2or3.html).

When doing `conda install python=3.5` in a 3.4 environment, this only updates python and some core packages, but not all packages. That means that most packages will still be in the 3.4 version and not found. Running `ipython` for example will tell you there is no `IPython` module - because the file in the `bin` folder still exists, but the module is not installed in the 3.5 module path.

I think the expected behavior of `conda install python=3.5` is that all python packages are updated to their 3.5 version.
What I did now is `conda update anaconda` which will update at least the anaconda packages. I probably still have some 3.4 packages in my 3.5 environment now, though.

issue_title: Size of the Scipy library on disk
issue_body:
At present Scipy takes 290 MB of disk space on Windows 8.1 x64 (using Python 3.4 and conda 3.16) and went up by almost 80 MB between Scipy versions 0.15 and 0.16 because of the addition of two Cython extensions: `scipy.linalg.cython_lapack` and `scipy.linalg.cython_blas`  with ~ 40 MB of disk space for each.

As a a comparison, the scipy wheel on pypi only takes 18 MB, a full install on Linux x64 takes 36 MB, and one with conda on Linux x64 96 MB, which is much more reasonable. In the later case, the `cython_lapack` and `cython_blas` only take 500 kB. 

I understand that on Windows, necessary libraries are statically liked in the produced `.pyd` file. However this feels like the conda install includes those files multiple times (i.e. for every cython or C extension). Can something be done about it? 

This particularly poses a problem when one has to make standalone executables with Pyinstaller or py2exe and the resulting `.exe` files become huge.

issue_title: Cannot interpolate variable with string value into eval query
issue_body:
This fails:

```
>>> state = b'California'
>>> bcolz.eval('STATE==state', user_dict={'state': state, 'STATE': tmp})
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<pyshell#15>", line 1, in <module>
    bcolz.eval('STATE==state', user_dict={'state': state, 'STATE': tmp})
  File "c:\users\brenbarn\documents\python\extensions\bcolz\bcolz\chunked_eval.py", line 131, in eval
    raise ValueError("only numpy/carray sequences supported")
ValueError: only numpy/carray sequences supported
```

But this works:

```
>>> bcolz.numexpr.evaluate(b"STATE == state", local_dict={'state': state, 'STATE': tmp})
array([False, False, False, ..., False, False, False], dtype=bool)
```

In other words, bcolz is being too aggressive about rejecting `state` as a comparison target; numexpr can handle it just fine.

There seem to be several places in the code where bcolz uses `hasattr(obj, '__len__')` as a way of deciding whether something is a scalar or not.  This isn't a good way to do that, because a single string has a length, but should be considered a scalar.  What is needed is something like pandas `is_array_like`, which explicitly special-cases string types.
make lomond available to the [anaconda repo](https://anaconda.org/anaconda/repo)

issue_title: --timestring flag supports only zero-padded decimal numbers
issue_body:
Hello,

In the documentation of --timestring there's a link to python strftime formatting (https://docs.python.org/2/library/datetime.html#strftime-and-strptime-behavior), but it supports only zero-padded decimal numbers, so if I have index named with string like "listener-3-8-2015" the curator command:

curator --dry-run delete indices --timestring '%d-%m-%Y' --prefix 'listener-' --older-than 60 --time-unit 'days'

wont catch it...
It seems like it wont support the command:

 --timestring '%-d-%-m-%Y' 

as described in http://strftime.org/

issue_title: Consider releasing new Python package names for major version changes
issue_body:
I've chatted with @HonzaKral about this in the past, but with the 5.X series unfortunately we are having a lot of issues getting the elasticsearch-py package 5.X versions to talk nicely to 1.X clusters so I wanted to re-surface this suggestion (whereas elasticsearch-py 2.X versions mostly talked fine to 2.X and 1.X clusters).

Context: If an organization has a large Python code base that needs to communicate with multiple versions of Elasticsearch, it's really hard to do with the current packaging strategy of elasticsearch-py because you can't install multiple versions of the same library in the same virtualenv. In the past we've gotten away with this because orgs were using other libraries (e.g. pyes) to communicate with 0.X and then elasticsearch-py for 1.X; so you could install both in the same virtualenv.

Proposal: I propose that since elasticsearch-py already does branch based development and uses relative imports, major version changes publish packages under a different package name, e.g. elasticsearch5. This way large python projects can import as many major versions of the client library as they want and not worry about cross version compatibility.

Tradeoffs: This solution mainly translates to some maintenance overhead in tests, where the code actually references the package name. Since elasticsearch-py already uses branches, this ought be relatively straightforward. You can also still publish master to "elasticsearch" maintaining backwards compatibility, or if it's annoying to apply patches use symlinks and tell setuptools explicitly which packages to build (this wouldn't work for tests).

Alternatives: Last time I chatted with Honza he suggested two alternatives. The first is to just use the latest version of elasticsearch-py to talk to all versions and it would probably mostly work because the bodies are what change version to version. For Elasticsearch 2.X this worked out ok, but Elasticsearch 5.X appears to have introduced some gnarly if-else complexity around node status endpoints and the like [example](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/pull/482/files) and our integration tests are definitely complaining about elasticsearch-py 5.X's ability to communicate with 1.X clusters especially around the fields/store_fields/source_fields search API change. The second alternative he suggested was a 'compat' namespace using AddonClient. I think this alternative could definitely work, but I feel like there is a lot of maintenance overhead for customers running multiple versions (having to maintain the compatibility layer).

Totally cool with the answer being "let's just have the customers that need it do namespaces with AddonClient" or "we'll put the if-else logic in elasticsearch-py", but I just wanted to raise the suggestion again. I think there is a lot of good prior art in system packaging (apache vs apache2), java packaging (shaded jars and package relocation) and even Python packaging (boto2 vs boto3) to support the usefulness of changing the package name for major version changes.

https://github.com/Yelp/elasticsearch-py/pull/1 was my go at this in our fork, took me about 15 minutes to relocate the package with the help of sed (also fwiw well done elasticsearch-py is a super simple library!).

issue_title: Notice: Breaking changes with CLI commands

Hello blobxfer users,

Due to the migration to Click for the CLI in 1.0.0 of blobxfer, the existing argument and parameter structure will change from the 0.x.y releases.

You can mitigate this now by verisoning your pip install, e.g., `pip install blobxfer==0.12.1`).

Thanks for your understanding as we improve the overall blobxfer experience and feature set.

issue_title: Azure Batch durations reported in wrong format in JSON
issue_body:
### Description

When you do a 'get' or 'list' of Azure Batch resources containing durations, the resulting JSON contains a textual description of the duration rather than the round-trippable, machine-processable representation used in the API.  E.g.

```
C:\temp>az batch job show --job-id cmd-line
{
  "constraints": {
    "maxTaskRetryCount": 0,
    "maxWallClockTime": "10675199 days, 2:48:05.477581"  // <------- not useful
  },
  "creationTime": "2016-10-26T02:29:14.820357+00:00",
  "displayName": null,
  "eTag": "0x8D3FD47E0FA3E0A",
...
```

This is particularly problematic for the value shown, which actually represents "no maximum wall clock time" using the implementation detail of .NET TimeSpan.MaxValue, and should therefore probably be omitted from the JSON.  But for non-default durations, or if we must present the default duration, it should be in PnnnTmmm format (ISO 8601).

issue_title: webapp deployment source config-zip use async api
issue_body:
Currently the `az webapp deployments source config-zip` command uses the sync version of the api endpoint `api/zipdeploy` on the webapp

This results in a small issue when deploying this way, because apparently the load balancer in front of the `.scm.azurewebsites.net` has a request timeout of 230 seconds, as mentioned by David Ebbo [in this issue on kudu](https://github.com/projectkudu/kudu/issues/2474).

We have quite a few times run into an issue where this leads to us getting a status code 500 back from a deploy, while the deploy was still finishing in the web app.

We have in our projects changed away from using the azure cli, to using a hand crafted bash script that uses the async version of the api as referenced in the documentation [here]https://github.com/projectkudu/kudu/wiki/REST-API#zip-deployment).

I think the `az webapp deployments source config-zip` command could benefit from using the `?isAsync=true` query parameter, to stop getting unnecessary and untrue errors.

issue_title: `application provision` returns not helpful 500 on client errors
issue_body:
If an application package is malformed or invalid, the `application provision` API will fail with a 500 response code. This is indicative of a server side error, rather than client and returns no meaningful information about what could be wrong with the application package.

A more detailed response would help reduce debugging times for provision failures.

issue_title: Storage queue service should automatically encode/decode base 64
issue_body:
The other SDKs (.NET, Node.js) do that, so if you want to interop with them, it's currently the user's responsibility to do the encoding/decoding when using the Python SDK.

We should make it transparent and automatically encode/decode base64 when sending/receiving queue messages.

This would be a breaking change.

If we don't do this, we should fix the docs at http://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/storage-python-how-to-use-queue-storage to show the encoding/decoding code required on the client's side.

Proposal: redesign machine with a task queue abstraction where a single task is processing one source. Cache, download, conform, extract a single source at a time. Doesn't require a fundamental rewrite, mostly refactoring existing code. Assumes all code running in Python, although shelling out to Node or ogr2ogr should be possible.

The current system runs all sources in sequential stages in a single Python process. `process.py` invokes code in `run.py` to first `run_all_caches`, then `run_all_conforms`, then `run_all_excerpts`. It then generates an HTML report from all the status objects. This works fine but has some drawbacks. The whole run currently takes 10 hours and will only take longer as more sources are added. Work is lost if the job fails, particularly awkward with EC2 spot instances. The code mixes application logic with thread and process logic.

I think it makes more sense to slice the work the other way, process each source as an independent job. The sources are themselves independent (different servers, different schemas, etc). It will make it easier to do test runs on a single source. It will make it easier to only re-run a conform if the source changed. It also presents a nice atomic unit of work to a task scheduling abstraction.

Details below.

##### Task

A single task processes a single user-contributed data source file. A task consists of several subtasks to perform sequentially:

* Download from source URL / ESRI store. (Optionally cache it; see below.)
* Conform the data, transforming the source to the OpenAddress CSV schema.
* Extract the data, presenting a few lines from the source for user inspection.
* Communicate task execution stats to the reporting system.

I propose each subtask be written as a Python module. Subtasks should also be able to run standalone on a local machine for development. Each subtask should have tests from a synthetic data source.

##### Source caching

The current Node code downloads data from the official source and caches it to S3, and is intelligent about only downloading if the data changed. Mike indicated that S3 dependence makes development challenging. But it's also useful in the (frequent) case the source goes down; see issue #9. I propose retaining an S3 cache in the system, but make the subtask functions able to work from S3 or local files.

##### Task Scheduling

The simplest scheduler is none at all, just run a Python loop over all sources with some sort of basic threading or multiprocessing. That will be functionally equivalent to where we are today, a single monolithic Python process.

We can then migrate to a task queue written to a persistent store. A job wakes up every minute to check if any sources have changed and if so, posts a processing tasks to the queue. Another job wakes up every minute to run tasks on the queue. Is there some AWS-friendly task abstraction we can reuse rather than writing our own?

##### Reporting

Reporting re-centralizes all the tasks when they are complete. The current system is a batch job sort of report, "I ran all the sources and the result on Dec 13 is X." I think it'd be better to move to a rolling report where each task updates its own little task stats record, then the reporter just has to group all task statuses together and publish an HTML report of the latest status of everything. But that is a product change.

##### Migration Plan

I'd start with a simple Python loop scheduler. This means rewriting `process.py:process()` to loop over sources. Most of `jobs.py` would be removed. I think even at this first stage we need some parallelism; perhaps process.py can simply fork a subprocess for each task? The current code for tasks and subtasks mostly exists, just needs to be refactored a bit. (`conform.py` is not yet complete.) The task status reporting requires some thinking.

Once that redesign is working as well as the current setup we can then move on to some more ambitious job scheduling system. I think that choice should be driven by what works best with AWS.

I also think it'd be worth re-examining the logic around caching data in S3 to make sure it's doing the right thing.

*It's hubris of me to propose a redesign; I wrote this up after some conversations with Mike about the direction he was moving in.*

issue_title: nodeenv try to download non existing tar.gz prebuilt under Cygwin
issue_body:
Hi,

Strange issue: I suspect a recent change broke this as it used to work last week, on another Windows computer with Cygwin.

Bug reproduction: `pre-commit run` using e.g. https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks-html v1.1.0

`pre-commit` execute the following command under the hood, a command that also fails if I execute it manually:
```
nodeenv --prebuilt /cygdrive/c/Users/admin/.pre-commit/repoYHJ85q/node_env-default
```
The error is the following:
```
urllib2.HTTPError: HTTP Error 404: Not Found
```
The `tar.gz` it tries to install is https://nodejs.org/dist/v7.2.1/node-v7.2.1-cygwin_nt-6.1-x64.tar.gz, which does not exist. My guess is that `nodeenv` should use the Windows prebuilts instead: https://nodejs.org/dist/v7.2.1/node-v7.2.1-win-x64.zip This is because `platform.system()` is used: https://github.com/ekalinin/nodeenv/blob/master/nodeenv.py#L503

I'm going to ask for help on the https://github.com/ekalinin/nodeenv project, but do you have any hint at what the root cause could be here ?

Running in Git Bash and Git Shell with pre-commit 0.4.1 both fail this way.
```
$ pre-commit run --all-files

iled←[0m

```

issue_title: Should btBroadphaseInterface destructor remove itself from btCollisionObject broadphase proxies?
issue_body:
I don't know Bullet very well so apologies if this doesn't make sense.  But, I just fixed a crash-on-exit bug in a program that uses Bullet.  The problem turned out to be that a object managing Bullet was using std::shared_ptrs to keep btBroadphaseInterface and btCollisionWorld pointers, among other things.  I noticed that the problem was fixed if I explicitly destroyed the btCollisionWorld object instead of letting the shared_ptrs take care of it.

It turns out that during the btCollisionWorld destructor, it makes some dereferences of a broadphase proxy that references the btBroadphaseInterface.  However, due to the order of the variables declared in the manager class, the btBroadphaseInterface was getting destroyed first, leading to a segfault during the btCollisionWorld destructor.

I was wondering if an acceptable solution would be for btBroadphaseInterface to remove any proxies that point to it during its destructor?  That way there would be no order dependency between their destructors.  However, maybe it's impossible, or even wasteful, since it would require a sweep through all broadphase objects just before they are destroyed.  Otherwise, any ideas on how such a segfault could be warned against or avoided?

Hello @erwincoumans,

First, I would like to thank you for this amazing piece of work you did (almost alone!).
I saw that recently you started to use Python (a very good language btw). I'm a fan of Python so I'm enjoying this direction.

From what I saw, you are directly using the CPython C API to create your extension module. This way offers you a great liberty but at a great cost, ultimate dependency with CPython. As you may know, Python can run with others interpreters than CPython, the most famous one is PyPy and offers better performance than CPython under certain circumstances.

The problem is that PyPy doesn't natively support the CPython C API (which is deeply linked with CPython internal code) and suffers of bad performance when using Python extension written for CPython. Fortunately, there is a solution which is very good: cffi.

CFFI is a Python module that allows you to easily write Python extension with full compatibility over CPython and PyPy. Moreover, it's a lot easier to write an extension with cffi than with CPython C API.

I would like to know if you ever considerate cffi as a solution or if maybe you don't know it.
I look forward to see your though about it.

Thanks in advance for your answer,
Sincerely,
Jean-Sébastien

issue_title: pybullet resetJointState jitters and disables collision
issue_body:
I know it's not ideal to call resetJointState every frame, but i dont know another way.

I'm trying to simulate my robot with urdf multibody. The robot has position sensors on the joints. I'd like to read the joint sensors and then update the multibody representation in bullet.

When i call resetJointState, the joint starts to jitter and it no longer has collision response. I tried to look through the code where this happens but i dont see any reason why it would stop responding to collision. It seems to just do an FK pass and then update the collision world transform. I dont see anywhere that would cause it to clear contacts or something.

Is there any way to override the joint positions in multibody and still have it treat the whole robot with proper dynamics and collision?

To be specific, the root link is not fixed, like the minitar. If you call resetJointState on the minitaur legs i suspect he will fall through the floor.

I thought maybe I could drive the links to positions with torques, but i dont want to apply torque to the system, i just want to "warp" the joints into position with as minimal impact on the chain as possible. 

Thank you!

By the way, i'm a long time fan of bullet. :)

issue_title: GET requests sometimes works with HTTP/1.0 sometimes with HTTP/1.1
issue_body:
## Long story short

I have an endpoint for simple health check ( `GET /health_check` ) which just returns `web.json_response(text="OK", status=200)`. HAProxy calls this but requires protocol HTTP/1.0 to be mentioned , when request comes with default HTTP/1.1 , it gives 405 Method not allowed error.

If I curl to the endpoint from some other machine , it works fine with HTTP/1.1 .  Now with HAProxy hitting my server with HTTP/1.0 results in a new connection everytime which should not happen since its just a health check. 

Something wrong in my configuration ?

## Expected behaviour

Hitting the endpoint with the GET request should work fine irrespective of HTTP 1.0/1.1 . Behaviour should not be ambiguous.

## Actual behaviour

Sometimes GET works with HTTP 1.1 and sometimes requires 1.0 . It should always work with 1.1

## Your environment

aiohttp==1.2.0
Python 3.5.2 
Running with Supervisord

## Long story short

Currently we need to write rather long line just to get reverse url:

``` python
async def handler(request):
    url = request.app.router['users'].url(**kw)
```

I propose to add shortcut function:

``` python

url_for(request, name, **kw)
```

issue_title: Notebook with Hadoop YARN webproxy (how to redirect static file serving)
issue_body:
Hey community!

A question related to serving notebook inside Hadoop yarn container. Hadoop YARN has this concept of Web Application Proxy, which to provide a protection against web based attacks.

So we render notebook inside a node say: **http://nodeA:12345**, the Web Proxy will hide the actual address and serve: **http://webproxy:8080/proxy/applicationId/**.  A request to noteA:12345/static/ will be mapped to http://webproxy:8080/proxy/applicationId/static/. However, notebook seems to hard static file serving path to /static. As a result of that when user queries static resources, we get:

GET http://webproxy:8080/static/tree/js/main.min.js?v=4139e87e4344862dcb29f94ad26c84cd 404 (Not Found)
?proxyapproved=true:131 GET http://webproxy:8080/static/base/images/logo.png?v=641991992878ee24c6f3826e81054a0f 404 (Not Found)

The working path should be http://webproxy:8080/proxy/applicationId/static/tree/js/main.min.js

I'm curious if this is configurable?

issue_title: Printing backspace characters does not line up with terminal behavior
issue_body:
I am using Python 3.5. and Jupyter Notebook 5.1 (although I have reproduced in try.jupyter.org which uses Python 3.6 and Jupyter Notebook 5.0 too).

For some reason, it looks like the way that the Jupyter notebook printing the `\b` is timing dependent: 

```python
import time
s = 0.1

print("the", end="")
time.sleep(s)

print("\b", end="", flush=False)
time.sleep(s)

print("\b", end="", flush=False)
time.sleep(s)
print("\b", end="")
```

If I set `s = 0.5`, then this snippet prints out `the` and before deleting it. But If I set `s = 0.1`, then only the `e` is deleted and I see `th` printed out (see https://gist.github.com/alanhdu/7604d67dfc755743a62ba42848842009).

This is also weird, because in the terminal (with both the `ipython` and `python` shell), I have to do `print("\b" * 3 + " " * 3 + "\b" * 3, end="")` to actually delete `the` (the `\b` just moves the cursor, the spaces clear it, and then `\b` to moves back to the beginning again), so neither behavior from the notebook lines up with the behavior from the console.

issue_title: Update sort buttons to work without hard dependency on font-awesome
New base theme not based on bootstrap
issue_body:
Bootstrap is a good css library, but we see that basing the theme on bootstrap has a few issues:

- We do not use very much of the components, and we customize them a lot.
- We have to work around a lot of the core components, such as container. This leads to some ugly hacks that are hard to maintain.
- We want to use BEM http://getbem.com/introduction/.
- We do not want to be bound to the bootstrap release cycle. Bootstrap major versions are fairly compatible, but far from completely compatible. We need major rewrites for each new version because we have so many overrides and customized components that depend on the bootstrap structure, and we have the names of bootstrap css classes all over the code (and these can change).

issue_title: Classes instead of instances in ModelType
issue_body:
I don't like the way the current ModelType paradigm works.

The syntax for defining a `ModelType` is to list the _classes_ and not _instances_ of the class. This is less flexible.

In PR [#340](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/340) I introduced a `PolyType` which will use a list of validators from type instances each with their own restraints, like `min_length` in the snippet below. Using classes, instead of instances, prevents this level of flexibility. I prefer to change the syntax for using `ModelTypes` than reduce functionality in the new `PolyType`.

From the snippet in PR 340:

```python
class Foo(Model):
    bla = ListType(PolyType([IntType(), StringType(min_length=3)]))
```

And the behavior of the `ModelType`:

```python
class Band(Model):
    name = StringType()

class Person(Model):
    band =  ModelType(Band) # this is the offending line
```

I would prefer `band = ModelType(Band())`

Another consideration is that default data would be possible for ModelTypes now too. I don't have a specific use case for this piece, but it is an interesting consequence of making the system internally consistent.

issue_title: [Feature] Ability to distinguish missing field from null-valued field
issue_body:
## Below is the current behaviour

```python
>>> from schematics.models import Model
>>> from schematics.types import StringType
>>> 
>>> 
>>> class UserSelfUpdateSerializer(Model):
...     name = StringType()
...     password = StringType()
...     email = StringType()
...     phone_number = StringType()
...     avatar = StringType()
... 
>>> 
>>> serializer = UserSelfUpdateSerializer({"password": "123"})
>>> serializer.to_native()
{'name': None, 'password': '123', 'email': None, 'phone_number': None, 'avatar': None}
```

Note that the serializer's exported data contains other four fields with None value.

## I want

I want an ability of not including missing fields while exporting data via `to_native` or `to_primitive`, **unless the input payload carries a field with None value explicitly**.

The future could like below:

```python
>>> serializer = UserSelfUpdateSerializer({"password": "123", "avatar": None})
{'password': '123', 'avatar': None}
```

## Why it's helpful?

Please see how I am implementing the *update-user-profile* endpoint in a RESTful service:

```python
@app.route('/users/current', methods=['PATCH'])
@id_required()
async def update_current_user(request):
    """
    Update user profile of currently identified user
    """
    if not request.json:
        raise InvalidUsage('JSON payload required!')

    user = await get_request_user(request)

    try:
        serializer = UserSelfUpdateSerializer(request.json)
        serializer.validate()
    except schematics.exceptions.DataError as e:
        errors = build_api_error_body(e)
        raise APIException(errors=errors, status_code=400)

    # Dangerous! serializer.name is None while no request.json['name'],
    # This way user.name will be unexpectly set NULL!
    user.import_data(serializer.to_primitive())
    await app.db.execute(
        user_tbl.update()
        .where(user_tbl.c.id == user.id)
        .values(user.to_native('self_update'))
    )

    return json(user.to_primitive('self_view'))
```

Note the comment starts with **Dangerous!**.
The user may only want to update one field, with others fields unchanged.

To make the code safe, I must replace this line:

```python
user.import_data(serializer.to_primitive())
```

to below:

```python
user.import_data({k: v for (k, v) in serializer.to_primitive().items() if k in request.json})
```

Which is much tedious and error-prone.

## Implementation recommendations

- [Marshmallow's Field.missing option](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html?highlight=missing#module-marshmallow.fields)
- How about introduce a new Model option to toggle new behaviour? e.g. `Model.missing_as_none`, default: True
- How about add an `missing_as_none` parameter to `Model.to_native/to_primitive` method? default: True

issue_title: Show status message during upload initial comparison
issue_body: When uploading large or many files ddsclient doesn't notify the user of what is going on until the uploading process starts. This can make it seem like ddsclient is hung when we are waiting for the list of files or calculating checksums.

The way the "stack" operation is currently implemented (including row_stack and column_stack) it invokes array(x) for each item of the input. This creates an object array (because the input is not an ndarray, it's an ArrayNode), which given the input array might have millions of entries is very very slow.

You can achieve the same thing (in the case of column_stack) with concatenate followed by reshape, so it seems odd that the stack function behaves so much worse. At least if it's expected it might be worth a note in the documentation.

Here's an example of the problem:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
import autograd

def test_one(x):
    a = np.sin(x)
    b = np.cos(x)
    c = np.concatenate((a, b)).reshape((-1, 2))
    return np.sum(c)

def test_two(x):
    a = np.sin(x)
    b = np.cos(x)
    c = np.column_stack((a, b))
    return np.sum(c)

x = np.zeros(1000000)

# both are quick
print(test_one(x))
print(test_two(x))

# this is quick
test_one_grad = autograd.grad(test_one)
print(test_one_grad(x))

# this never terminates
test_two_grad = autograd.grad(test_two)
print(test_two_grad(x))
```

issue_title: [Doubt] Would iteratively setting a non-local variable as the answer result in correct gradients
issue_body:
Suppose I had a class method that like:
```python
def call(self, X):
    for t in range(X.shape[0]):
        self.ans = some_function(X[t], self.ans)       # some_function is a parameterized operation
        # some more computation with self.ans
        # final step has a scalar loss function
```

would I get correct gradients of this whole process? Meaning - will the computation graph that autograd constructs have the value of `self.ans` stored per iteration and use it?

issue_title: Add "How to make a release" instructions
issue_body:
Here is a draft adapted from:
* https://github.com/girder/girder/blob/master/docs/development.rst
* https://hynek.me/articles/sharing-your-labor-of-love-pypi-quick-and-dirty/

```
Creating a new release
----------------------

Scikit-build releases are uploaded to `PyPI <https://pypi.python.org/pypi/scikit-build>`_
for easy installation via ``pip``. In addition, the python source packages
are stored as releases inside the official
`github repository <https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/releases>`_. The
recommended process for generating a new release is described here.

1.  From the target commit, set the desired version number in ``setup.py``. 
    Create a new commit and note the SHA; this will become the release tag.

2.  Ensure that all tests pass. (AppVeyor, CircleCI and TravisCI should be green)

3.  Clone the repository in a new directory and checkout the release SHA.
    (Packaging in an old directory could cause files to be mistakenly included.)

4.  Run ``python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel``.  This will generate the source
    distribution tarball and wheel in the ``dist`` directory with a name like
    ``scikit-build-<version>.tar.gz`` and ``scikit_build-<version>-py2.py3-none-any.whl``.

5.  Create a new virtual environment and install the python package into
    it and build the web client. This should not be done in the repository
    directory because the wrong ``scikit-build`` package would be imported.  ::

        mkdir test && cd test
        virtualenv release
        source release/bin/activate
        pip install ../dist/scikit_build-<version>-py2.py3-none-any.whl

6.  Now you can try to import skbuild.

        python -c "import skbuild"

7. Create a ``~/.pypirc`` 
    consisting of: ::

        [distutils]
        index-servers=
            test

        [test]
        repository = https://testpypi.python.org/pypi
        username = <your user name goes here>
        password = <your password goes here>


Then use ::

        python setup.py register -r test

to register your project with the PyPI test server.

And finally, upload the release to PyPI test server using ``twine``: ::

        pip install twine
        twine upload -r test -s dist/scikit*

9. Test that the release can be installed: ::

        deactivate
        virtualenv install
        source install/bin/activate
        pip install -i https://testpypi.python.org/pypi scikit-build

10. When you are confident everything is working correctly, upload the release
    on PyPI:

First, update ``~/.pypirc``:

        [distutils]
        index-servers=
            pypi
            test

        [pypi]
        repository = https://pypi.python.org/pypi
        username = <your production user name goes here>
        password = <your production password goes here>

        [test]
        repository = https://testpypi.python.org/pypi
        username = <your user name goes here>
        password = <your password goes here>

Second, register the project with the PyPI server: ::

        python setup.py register

And upload the release: ::

        twine upload -s dist/scikit*


11.  Add the tagged version to `readthedocs <https://readthedocs.org/projects/scikit-build/>`_.

```
Make sure the hard fork activates correctly, and new features come online at the right block.

issue_body: @fnothaft has concerns (upon which he will elaborate) regarding the testing process.

@hannes-ucsc and I discussed this after scrum today.

In mock-up mode, the steps of the pipeline should do the following, given a small fastq and associated bam file (<20mb):

BWA alignment: Downloads input fastq and pre-computed mock.bam file. Renames bam file and writes to the appropriate s3 bucket.
ADAM preprocessing: Runs as usual, but using tiny instances. Produces a preprocessed bam file which is then replaced with an empty bam that is written to s3.
GATK preprocessing: Downloads the input BAM file, writes dummy intermediate files to the jobstore, and writes a blank preprocessed bam file with the appropriate name to s3.
Haplotype caller: Downloads the input preprocessed BAM file, and writes a blank vcf file with the appropriate name to s3.

In addition we will add assertions after the completion of each pipeline step that check that all necessary files are present. These checks will run in both mock-up mode and production mode.

@fnothaft what are your thoughts? I can get started on this today if you think it seems reasonable.
Or maybe fix it. Currently, it is just noise.

issue_title: Add async finalizers to the request-context
issue_body:
Hi,
My project is Python 3.5 only, and it's therefore impossible for me to use async generators in fixtures. But we might be able to workaround this if it were possible to define async finalizers to `request`.

E.g.
```python
@pytest.fixture
async def foo_fixture(request):
    async def fin():
        await some_stuff()
    request.addasyncfinalizer(fin)
    return 42
```
Then it would await for each async finalizers before tear down.

Not sure whether it would involve monkey patching on the request object, or what would be acceptable. Maybe there are also more proper solutions that doesn't involve `request` or that make use of already existing features? I already tried to use `loop.create_task()` in a standard finalizer, unfortunately, the created task seem to execute after teardown which produces the following error message:
`2017-06-26 18:15:50,262 - asyncio - ERROR - Task was destroyed but it is pending!`

Many thanks!

issue_title: Strange Tm behaviour for probe design
issue_body:
Dear Primer3-py,

I'm trying to make Primer3 find probe sequences for me, but I'm experiencing some weird behaviour with the temperature for the probe. If I use the default parameters for PRIMER_INTERNAL_MIN_TM=57, PRIMER_INTERNAL_OPT_TM=60, and PRIMER_INTERNAL_MAX_TM=63, then I experience that the predicted Tm from the bindings.designPrimers call is different than the ThermoAnalysis.calcTm for the probe sequence:
```
                    oligo   tm designPrimers  tm ThermoAnalysis
     AGCTGAACATACACGGCACA               60.0               60.0
   CGACGACGAACACCACTAGGCG               59.9               65.5
     GGTGGGTAAGCTTGCCAGTA               59.7               59.7
```
As you can see, both primers get the same prediction, but the probe Tm is 65,5, which is above the max threshold. 

Kind regards,
Martin

User story: As a developer, I can query a compose over HTTP and retrieve a list of all builds in the compose.

Currently I can instantiate a Compose object like so:
```
    compose = productmd.compose.Compose(path)
    ... (and then query the list of builds, etc)
```

The problem is that "`path`" must be a local or NFS path, like `/mnt/redhat/path/to/my/compose`.

Within Red Hat, some of our NFS shares are restricted across data centers, and the composes are only accessible via HTTP. It would be great to improve productmd to support querying over HTTP instead of NFS.
It would be great if Pungi wrote official productmd metadata about the container(s) it builds in Koji.

The final version of the library for the 2.6.1 release is more or less what's on master now, I just need to update the NEWS file. Here is the current version of the NEWS file, should ping those who were involved. If anyone wants their github ID replaced with a real name or anyone notices any issues, please let me know. I'll be tagging and releasing soon.

- Updated zoneinfo file to 2017b. (gh pr #395)
- Added Python 3.6 to CI testing (gh pr #365)
- Removed duplicate test name that was preventing a test from being run.
  Reported and fixed by @jdufresne (gh pr #371)
- Fixed testing of folds and gaps, particularly on Windows (gh pr #392)
- Fixed deprecated escape characters in regular expressions. Reported by
  @nascheme and @thierryba (gh issue #361), fixed by @thierryba (gh pr #358)
- Many PEP8 style violations and other code smells were fixed by @jdufresne
  (gh prs #358, #363, #364, #366, #367, #368, #372, #374, #379, #380)
- Improved performance of tzutc and tzoffset objects. (gh pr #391)
- Fixed issue with several time zone classes around DST transitions in any
  zones with +0 standard offset (e.g. Europe/London) (gh issue #321, pr #390)
- Fixed issue with fuzzy parsing where tokens similar to AM/PM that are in the
  end skipped were dropped in the fuzzy_with_tokens list. Reported and fixed
  by @jbrockmendel (gh pr #332).
- Fixed issue with parsing dates of the form X m YY. Reported by @jbrockmendel.
  (gh issue #333, pr #393)
- Added support for parser weekdays with less than 3 characters. Reported by
  @arcadefoam (gh issue #343), fixed by @jonemo (gh pr #382)
- Fixed issue with the addition and subtraction of certain relativedeltas.
  Reported and fixed by @kootenpv (gh issue #346, pr #347)
- Fixed issue where the COUNT parameter of rrules was ignored if 0. Fixed by
  @mshenfield (gh pr #330), reported by @vaultah (gh issue #329).
- Updated documentation to include the new tz methods. (gh pr #324)
- Update documentation to reflect that the parser can raise TypeError, reported
  and fixed by @tomchuk (gh issue #336, pr #337)
- Fixed an incorrect year in a parser doctest. Fixed by @xlotlu (gh pr #357)

issue_title: schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117.tar.gz has unwanted files in it
issue_body:
I noticed this while testing a fresh install of Planemo where this was in the install output:

```
Searching for schema-salad==1.12.20160610104117
Reading https://pypi.python.org/simple/schema-salad/
Best match: schema-salad 1.12.20160610104117
Downloading https://pypi.python.org/packages/51/99/cae16cfd2e981702441011e572445e12fdd0f8f69070a8ee13d09109120b/schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117.tar.gz#md5=92635d8e1317aa161f4a35881076dc33
Processing schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117.tar.gz
Writing /tmp/easy_install-42E9pF/schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/setup.cfg
Running schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/setup.py -q bdist_egg --dist-dir /tmp/easy_install-42E9pF/schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/egg-dist-tmp-Jiro6f
  File "build/bdist.macosx-10.11-intel/egg/schema_salad/ref_resolver_BACKUP_28804.py", line 222
    <<<<<<< 973dceb3aeef33920968fa257c7b04885a3a716d
     ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

Adding schema-salad 1.12.20160610104117 to easy-install.pth file
Installing schema-salad-tool script to /usr/local/bin
```

Verified with a direct download of ``schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117.tar.gz`` from PyPI:

```
$ curl -L -O https://pypi.python.org/packages/51/99/cae16cfd2e981702441011e572445e12fdd0f8f69070a8ee13d09109120b/schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117.tar.gz#md5=92635d8e1317aa161f4a35881076dc33
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
                                 Dload  Upload   Total   Spent    Left  Speed
100 56403  100 56403    0     0   198k      0 --:--:-- --:--:-- --:--:--  198k
$ tar -zxvf schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117.tar.gz#md5=92635d8e1317aa161f4a35881076dc33
...
x schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/schema_salad/ref_resolver_BACKUP_28804.py
...
x schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/schema_salad/ref_resolver_BASE_28804.py
...
x schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/schema_salad/ref_resolver_REMOTE_28804.py
x schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/schema_salad/ref_resolver_LOCAL_28804.py
...
```

One of the four extra files, ``schema-salad-1.12.20160610104117/schema_salad/ref_resolver_BACKUP_28804.py`` file looks like a failed merge from commit 973dceb3aeef33920968fa257c7b04885a3a716d (Use scopedRef and typeDSL in metaschema), and is therefore not valid Python. e.g.

```
<<<<<<< 973dceb3aeef33920968fa257c7b04885a3a716d

=======
                if "refScope" in value:
                    self.scoped_ref_fields[key] = value["refScope"]
                if value.get("typeDSL"):
                    self.type_dsl_fields.add(key)
>>>>>>> Use scopedRef and typeDSL in metaschema
```

This is fixed in the subsequence release ``schema-salad-1.13.20160630144631.tar.gz`` (none of these extra files are present) but perhaps something in the build process can be tweaked to prevent this?

issue_title: Map only displays data with FeatureGroups if LayerControl is the last thing added
issue_body:
Hi, 

I´m working with simple layers with feature groups and noticed that if the call `m.add_child(folium.LayerControl())` is not the last one made before plotting the map, the data on the feature groups is not shown. 

For example, 

    m = folium.Map(location=[-23.66, -55.32])
    coords = [[-23.12, -46.2],[-23.87,-45.65],[-23.44,-46.11]]
    feat_group = folium.FeatureGroup()
    feat_group.add_child(plugins.HeatMap(coords))
    m.add_child(feat_group)
    m.add_child(folium.LayerControl()) # this call must be the last one
    m

things work fine, but if

    m = folium.Map(location=[-23.66, -55.32])
    coords = [[-23.12, -46.2],[-23.87,-45.65],[-23.44,-46.11]]
    m.add_child(folium.LayerControl()) # this call no is made here
    feat_group = folium.FeatureGroup()
    feat_group.add_child(plugins.HeatMap(coords))
    m.add_child(feat_group)
    m

no data is displayed. The same happens if the `Map` is defined as the return value of a function:

    def gen_map():
        map_line = folium.Map(location=[-23.66, -55.32])
        map_line.add_child(folium.LayerControl())
        return map_line

    m = gen_map()
    coords = [[-23.12, -46.2],[-23.87,-45.65],[-23.44,-46.11]]
    feat_group = folium.FeatureGroup()
    feat_group.add_child(plugins.HeatMap(coords))
    m.add_child(feat_group)
    #m.add_child(folium.LayerControl()) #doesn´t matter if this is called here or not, the data is not displayed
    m

Is is a bug?

issue_title: folium.choropleth() does not use pandas series as key column correctly
issue_body:
**Set-up:** To give some idea of the set-up, I have a 2-column pandas DataFrame, called `german_cars_price`. The first column of this dataframe is labeled `state` and denotes the relevant German federal state. The second column is labeled `price` and refers to the average used car sales price in that German federal state from a sample of ads on eBay.

I also have a GeoJSON file, `data/federal_state_borders.geojson`, which gives the boundaries of the German federal states, and I have imported that into Python using `geopandas`, to get a (geo) DataFrame called `federal_state_borders`. The column in this DataFrame identifying the German federal states is called `VAR_NAME1`. It is a modified version of the geoJSON found [here](https://github.com/isellsoap/deutschlandGeoJSON) (my translations of the names into English are different).

For the full set-up of a minimal working example producing this problem, see here: https://github.com/krinsman/minimal_working_example

The environment.yml and the Makefile to create the conda virtual environment from it is included for completeness, but it seems unlikely that this would be necessary in all cases.

**Problem:** Trying to create a choropleth map using this data leads to the error:    

    ValueError: The truth value of a Series is ambiguous. Use a.empty, a.bool(), a.item(), a.any() or a.all().

This seems to be an issue involving how the `color_fill` attribute is interpreted.

Here is the minimal working example of the code:

    import pandas as pd
    import numpy as np
    import folium
    import geopandas as gpd
    import os

    german_cars_price=pd.read_csv('data/german_cars_price.csv',index_col=0)
    federal_state_borders = gpd.read_file(os.path.join('data', 'federal_state_borders.geojson'))

    m = folium.Map(location=[52.5194, 13.4067], tiles='Mapbox Bright', zoom_start=5)
    m.choropleth(geo_data=federal_state_borders, key_on=federal_state_borders.VARNAME_1, data=german_cars_price, columns=['state', 'price'], fill_color='YlGn')

It seems fairly likely of course that I am doing something incorrectly, but it is unclear to me based on the documentation.

**Caveat lector:** this is probably my mistake as an end-user rather than a bug of the software.

I have not found the problem documented anywhere, and [the example in the documentation](http://python-visualization.github.io/folium/quickstart.html#Choropleth-maps) is too sparse (it does not specify the required formats of the data, or show what the structure of the arguments being passed to choropleth() in that call are) to deduce a solution. In particular, what `feature` is supposed to refer to, in either the example or in `folium.py`, is beyond me.

Is it possible to use a linear colormap when building a choropleth from pandas using the map.choropleth method? I've read the documentation and and the jupyter notebook docs, which explain how to use a linear colour map when building from a GeoJSON, but not when using the key_on and columns keywords.

Is there a way to replace the threshold_scale and fill_color args in my code and get an equivalent linear colour scale without breaks?

import folium

#Let Folium determine the scale
map = folium.Map(location=[53, -1], zoom_start=7)
map.choropleth(geo_path='./updated-file2.json',
               threshold_scale=[50,55,60,65,70,75],
               data=master, key_on='feature.properties.lad16nm', 
               legend_name='Average SAP Rating by region',
               fill_color='PRGn',columns=['NewName', 'SAPRatingAve'], highlight=True)
map.save('epcmap.html')

issue_title: Defaulting to 10 threads bad idea on consumer ADSL
issue_body:
In Australia, the vast majority of consumers have ADSL internet connections, with average upstream bandwidth around 0.7 mbit/sec. Yeah, I know, it sucks.

The b2 tool here seems to split big files into 100mbyte chunks, which it then tries to upload in up to ten simultaneous TCP streams.

This is disastrous. For an average Australian internet user, each of those streams will get 0.07 mbit/s each, or 7kbyte/sec. So it will take nearly four hours to complete -- and the b2 servers seem to give up and kick out connections that have lingered for a while.

Perhaps you could set threads to 1 by default, and suggest that users on high-bandwidth connections increase it? Or at least make it much clearer in the README that threads defaults to 10, which may be wildly inappropriate for low-bandwidth users?

Thanks!

The new API call [b2_get_download_authorization](https://www.backblaze.com/b2/docs/b2_get_download_authorization.html) is currently not supported anywhere, not even at the `raw_api` level. I'm not sure if it's useful to have in the command line tool, but having it in the API certainly would.

issue_title: API Updates instead of Creates if there is a single object in the database
issue_body:
I have a create endpoint for a certain object. If there is a single object already in the database, when I attempt to create another one with the API, it instead updates the single existing one.

I believe this is due to the code in lines 2091-2094 in resources.py. The logic goes that if there are not existing objects in the database it raises a DoesNotExist error. If there is more than one object in the database it raises as MultipleObjectsReturned error. But if there is a single object in the database, the single item in the database is returned.

![tastypie_create_objects_issue](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/408926/22163178/99f26194-df06-11e6-9a2e-40e8e59c6ef5.png)

This returns the object to the function put_detail (line 1407), which assumes that this is an update function on that single item instead of a create function.

Instead, I would expect it to check against the existence of a PK or ID on the bundle. Perhaps by using the detail_uri_name or something like that.

issue_title: Exception: Cannot get fields from a polymorphic serializer given a queryset
issue_body:
I'm sure I'm just doing something wrong but the documentation on working with polymorphic resources is pretty sparse so I'm not sure what to change. I have a polymorphic model `Client` with two instance types and when I try to retrieve them using DRF's ModelViewSet I get this error: `Exception: Cannot get fields from a polymorphic serializer given a queryset`.

Here are my views:

```python
from rest_framework import permissions, viewsets
from rest_framework.authentication import TokenAuthentication
from firms.models import Firm, Membership
from .serializers import ClientSerializer

class ClientViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    authentication_classes = (TokenAuthentication,)
    included = []
    pagination_class = None
    permission_classes = (permissions.IsAuthenticated,)
    serializer_class = ClientSerializer

    def perform_create(self, serializer):
        membership = Membership.objects.get(user_id=self.request.user.id)
        serializer.save(firm=membership.firm)

    def get_queryset(self):
        try:
            membership = Membership.objects.get(user_id=self.request.user.id)
            return membership.firm.clients.all()
        except Membership.DoesNotExist:
            return []
```

And here is my serializer:

```python
class CorporationSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Corporation
    .
    .
    .

class IndividualSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Individual
    .
    .
    .

class ClientSerializer(PolymorphicModelSerializer):
    polymorphic_serializers = [CorporationSerializer, IndividualSerializer]

    class Meta:
        model = Client
```

I have my `resource_name`s defined on my models. I've tried passing a list instead of a queryset and that works for an empty response (`[]`) but I run into issue #400 for non-empty responses. What should I be passing to my serializer?

issue_title: Unexpected behaviour in overwriting named config with command line when using ingredients
issue_body:
It is noted in the [docs](https://sacred.readthedocs.io/en/latest/command_line.html#combination-with-regular-updates) that config given as command line arguments overwrite named config and config files. This works as expected for root configuration entries, but the overwrite unexpectedly does not happen for configuration entries of an ingredient. This occurs for both named config functions and configuration files.

Consider file `sacredtest.py`:
```
from sacred import Experiment, Ingredient

ing = Ingredient('ingredient')
ex = Experiment('exp', ingredients=[ing])


@ex.named_config
def conf():
    ex_opt = 1
    ingredient = {'ingredient_opt': 2}


@ex.capture
def ex_f(ex_opt):
    print(ex_opt)


@ing.capture
def ingredient_f(ingredient_opt):
    print(ingredient_opt)


@ex.automain
def main():
    pass
```

ex_opt: 1
